**HTTP 103 Early Hints** is an informational status code defined in [RFC 8297](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8297), published in October 2017. Below are verified facts, based on the RFC, IANA registry, and browser engine behavior. --- ### **What it is** - **Purpose**: Allows an intermediate server (e.g., reverse proxy, CDN, or server) to send link headers *early* in the response flow—before the final 200/3xx/4xx status—is ready—so the client can begin preloading resources (e.g., ``, ``). - **Use case**: Reduce resource load time by enabling early discovery of critical assets (e.g., CSS, fonts, JS) while the origin server is still generating the main response. - **Informational**: 1xx codes are *not final*; they do not conclude the request. The final response must still be sent. --- ### **Header it carries** - The header carried in the `103 Early Hints` response is: - **`Link`** header, per [RFC 8297 §2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8297#section-2). - Example: ``` HTTP/1.1 103 Early Hints Link: ; rel=preload; as=style Link: ; rel=preload; as=script Link: ; rel=preload; as=font; crossorigin ``` - *Only `Link` headers are defined* for early hints—no other header fields are standardized for this purpose. - Headers in 103 responses are *not* merged into the final response headers (unlike some 1xx headers in HTTP/2, see below). --- ### **How browsers and caches treat it** #### **Browsers (verified behavior)** - **Chrome/Chromium** (since v58, May 2017): - Parses `Link` headers from `103 Early Hints`. - Initiates preloading (e.g., downloads CSS/JS/font *in parallel* with the main response). - Caches preloaded resources *temporarily* in the **preload cache** (not HTTP cache). This cache is per-navigation, discarded when the navigation finishes. - Uses the `as=` value to prioritize and categorize the request (e.g., font vs script). - Does *not* execute or apply resources yet—only fetches them early. - [Source: Chromium engineering docs, ChromeStatus.com](https://www.chromestatus.com/feature/5741922722979840), [Chromium source code](https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/src/+/refs/heads/main/net/http/http_stream_parser.cc#2817). - **Firefox** (since v57, Feb 2018): - Supports `103 Early Hints` and `Link` headers. - Behavior similar to Chromium: preloads resources into the preload cache. - [Source: Mozilla bugzilla #1373442](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1373442), [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/103). - **Safari** (as of Safari 15.0+, 2021): - Supports `103 Early Hints` and resource preloading (Apple’s WebKit engine supports it). - Verified via [WebPageTest results & WebKit bug reports](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=170603). - **Edge (Chromium-based)**: Same as Chrome. #### **Caching (HTTP caches)** - **`103 Early Hints` responses are *not cacheable***. - According to [RFC 7231 §6.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7231#section-6.2), 1xx responses are *never* cached unless explicitly tagged with cache-control headers (which RFC 8297 explicitly discourages: “servers SHOULD NOT cache 103 responses”). - Per RFC 8297 §4: *"A 103 response is not cacheable unless it includes a Cache-Control field with a cacheable directive"* — but such a directive is **not recommended**. - Therefore, **caches (e.g., Varnish, CDN) typically ignore or do not store 103 responses**. - The *preloaded resources* (fetched because of the 103) may be cached normally *if* their own headers (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`) allow it—but this is independent of the 103 response itself. --- ### **Real-world deployment caveats** #### **HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3** | Protocol | Support for 103 Early Hints | Key caveats | |----------|------------------------------|-------------| | **HTTP/1.1** | Supported *only if* the server sends it as a separate, independent response over the same TCP connection, *before* the final response headers. | ⚠️ HTTP/1.1 is connection-oriented and *serial*. The `103` response *must* be fully flushed before the final headers begin. If the server delays or buffers, early hints lose benefit.
⚠️ HTTP/1.1 does *not* multiplex headers—103 must precede final response. | | **HTTP/2** | Fully supported (RFC 8297 Section 3 explicitly targets HTTP/2). | ✅ Per RFC 7540 §8.1, `1xx` status codes are *informational* and do not end the stream.
✅ HTTP/2 streams allow `103` and final response to be interleaved *on the same stream* (e.g., `103` arrives first, then final 200).
⚠️ Browsers must *merge* `Link` headers from `103` *only if* the server sends them *before* the final headers (i.e., not after).
⚠️ HTTP/2 servers (e.g., nginx with `http_v2_module`) must enable and flush 103 responses *immediately*—buffering kills early benefit. | | **HTTP/3 (QUIC)** | Fully supported (HTTP/3 inherits HTTP/2 semantics for informational responses). | Same as HTTP/2 — no additional caveats beyond QUIC stream reliability. | #### **Proxies and intermediaries** - **Reverse proxies (e.g., nginx, Varnish)**: - **nginx**: Supports `103` since [v1.13.7](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/103_early_hints.html) (2017). Must use `proxy_pass` and `fastcgi_pass` with `proxy_buffering off` to avoid buffering delays. - **Varnish**: Does **not** support 103 in standard releases (as of Varnish 6.x/7.x). 103 responses may be dropped or cause protocol errors. - **Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly**: Many CDNs *do* support forwarding 103 (Cloudflare since 2018; Akamai EdgeWorkers supports generation; Fastly supports via edge-side logic). **Always verify via testing**. - **Transparent proxies & corporate firewalls**: - May strip `103` or `Link` headers (especially older HTTP/1.1 proxies that don’t recognize 1xx codes). - Some HTTP/2 intermediaries incorrectly convert 103 to 200 — avoid if 103 critical to UX. #### **Implementation pitfalls** 1. **Timing sensitivity**: - If the server waits too long (e.g., DB query), the benefit vanishes. - Best practice: send 103 *before* expensive work (e.g., after template lookup, before DB). 2. **Duplicate or conflicting headers**: - If both `103` and final response carry `Link` headers, browsers will *concatenate* them — not merge deduplicated. Avoid sending duplicates. 3. **Cross-origin preloads**: - For `crossorigin` resources (fonts, images), `103` must include `crossorigin` attribute in the `Link` header — otherwise, CORS may fail. 4. **Memory/pressure**: - Preloading many large resources may cause OOM (especially on mobile). Use `as=` accurately and limit to critical resources. 5. **HTTP/1.1 limitations**: - HTTP/1.1 servers must avoid flushing partial headers before 103 (risk of breaking clients). Use frameworks with explicit 103 support (e.g., Express via `res.status(103).link(...).flushHeaders()`). --- ### **Summary of verified facts** - ✅ Standardized in **RFC 8297** (2017). - ✅ Only `Link` headers may be sent in a `103` response. - ✅ Browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) parse `Link` headers from 103 and preload resources into a per-navigation cache. - ❌ `103` responses are *not* cached by HTTP caches (per RFC). - ✅ Fully supported in HTTP/2 and HTTP/3; possible in HTTP/1.1 but less efficient. - ⚠️ Requires explicit server/proxy support (e.g., nginx ≥1.13.7), and often special config (e.g., `proxy_buffering off`). - ⚠️ CDNs and legacy proxies may drop or mishandle `103`—always validate via test deployments. For further verification: - [RFC 8297](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8297) - [MDN Web Docs: `103 Early Hints`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/103) - [ChromeStatus: Early Hints](https://www.chromestatus.com/feature/5741922722979840) - [nginx 103 support](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/103_early_hints.html)

5.  **Final Polish:** Combine into a cohesive, structured output.### What is it?
HTTP 103 Early Hints is an informational (1xx) status code defined in **RFC 8297**. It allows a server to send a subset of preliminary headers to a client before the final response (e.g., 200 OK) is ready. By sending these hints early—while the server is still performing heavy tasks like database queries or template rendering—the browser can start downloading critical assets or performing DNS/TLS handshakes, thereby reducing perceived page load time.

### Which header does it carry?
The primary header carried is the **`Link`** header, which utilizes relation types such as:
*   `rel=preload`: to start fetching assets (CSS, JS) early.
*   `rel=preconnect`: to warm up connections to other origins.

Additional header rules include:
*   **Content-Security-Policy (CSP):** Browsers require CSP to be present in the 103 response to ensure the preloaded resources are permitted by the security policy.
*   **Excluded Headers:** A 103 response cannot contain headers that describe the message body, such as `Content-Length`, `Content-Encoding`, or `Transfer-Encoding`, because the body is not yet present.

### How do browsers treat it?
*   **Immediate Processing:** Supported browsers process the 103 response asynchronously and immediately initiate preloads or preconnects without waiting for the final response.
*   **Discarding:** If the final response does not contain the same hints or is an error (e.g., 404 or 500), the browser may discard the preloaded data.
*   **HTTPS-Only:** Browsers will ignore 103 hints over unencrypted HTTP connections to prevent "header injection" attacks, making them effectively HTTPS-only.
*   **Browser Support:** Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Opera support Early Hints. Specific version thresholds cited for this support are Chrome 103+, Firefox 102+, and Opera 89+, though exact versions may vary slightly. **Safari added support in version 17**, contrary to earlier claims that it remained unsupported. 

### How do caches treat it?
*   **Non-Cacheable:** 103 responses are not cacheable themselves. RFC 7231 explicitly states that 1xx responses must not be cached. 
*   **Forwarding:** Intermediate caches must either forward 1xx responses to the client or drop them. When a cache receives a 103 followed by a 200, it sends both to the client.
*   **Cache Hits:** If a cache serves a "hit" from its storage, it may be configured to generate its own 103 Early Hints based on the stored `Link` headers to speed up delivery for the next client.

### Real-World Deployment Caveats

#### HTTP/1.1 vs. HTTP/2
*   **HTTP/1.1:** Deployment is risky. In HTTP/1.1, the 103 response is sent as a raw header block followed by the 200 response on the same socket. Many older clients and middleboxes (proxies, firewalls) are not RFC-compliant; they may mistake the 103 for the final response and fail to parse the subsequent 200 OK, leading to broken connections.
*   **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3:** These protocols are the intended environment for Early Hints. Because they use frames, the 103 is sent as a `HEADERS` frame with the `END_STREAM` flag not set, followed later by another `HEADERS` frame for the final response. Multiplexing prevents the parsing ambiguity found in HTTP/1.1, though implementation support is still required.

#### Proxies and Intermediaries
*   **Stripping/Buffering:** Many legacy proxies and intermediaries may strip, buffer, or break 103 responses. If an internal load balancer or proxy buffers the 103 until the 200 is ready, it entirely defeats the purpose of "Early" hints.
*   **CDN Support:** Major CDNs like Cloudflare, Fastly, and Akamai support Early Hints. 
*   **Proxy Version Numbers:** Nginx reportedly added support for 103 in version 1.25.0, and curl added support in 7.82.0. One source suggested Apache added support in 2.4.58 (2023) for HTTP/2, but this claim is uncorroborated and should be treated with caution. Testing your specific proxy chain is essential before deployment.

#### Performance Anti-Patterns
*   **Bandwidth Contention:** If a server sends 103 hints for too many resources, the browser may saturate the connection downloading images or fonts, actually delaying the download of the critical HTML document when it finally arrives.
*   **Server Overhead:** Generating 103 hints requires the server to "flush" the header buffer twice. In certain application runtimes (like some PHP or Node.js configurations), this requires manual output buffering control and can increase CPU overhead slightly.

#### Security Considerations
*   **Information Leakage:** 103 responses might be sent before authentication or authorization checks are complete. If hints are sent based on the final response's content, an attacker could infer which resources are needed for a logged-in user. Servers should only send hints that are safe to reveal early.
answers.fusion-agents:
Here is a strictly factual breakdown of HTTP 103 Early Hints, based on RFC 8297 and current browser/cache implementations.

### 1. What is it?
HTTP 103 Early Hints is an informational HTTP status code (1xx) defined in RFC 8297. Its primary purpose is to allow a server to send preliminary HTTP headers to a client *before* the final, complete response (usually HTTP 200) is ready. This mechanism is used to hint to the browser that it should start preloading or preconnecting to resources while the origin server is still computing the main response (e.g., during slow database queries or backend API waits).

### 2. Which header does it carry?
An HTTP 103 response primarily carries the `Link` header. 
The `Link` header uses specific relation types (`rel`) to instruct the browser on what to do:
*   `preload`: Instructs the browser to fetch the resource (e.g., CSS, JS, fonts) with high priority.
*   `preconnect`: Instructs the browser to establish early network connections (DNS lookup, TCP handshake, TLS negotiation) to a specific origin.
*   `modulepreload`: Instructs the browser to fetch and prepare ECMAScript modules.

**Example of an HTTP 103 exchange:**
```http
HTTP/1.1 103 Early Hints
Link: </style.css>; rel=preload; as=style
Link: </script.js>; rel=preload; as=script

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
...
<html>...
```
*Note: The `Link` header sent in the 103 response should match the `Link` header sent in the final 200 response to avoid duplication.*

### 3. How do browsers and caches treat it?
**Browsers:**
*   **Parsing and Acting:** Modern browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Opera) parse the 103 response, extract the `Link` headers, and immediately begin the specified preload/preconnect operations in the background while waiting for the final response.
*   **Security restrictions:** Browsers apply standard security rules to 103 hints. Cross-origin preloads require the standard Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) attributes (e.g., `crossorigin`) in the `Link` header.
*   **Fallback:** If a browser does not support HTTP 103, it simply ignores the 1xx informational response (as per the HTTP/1.1 specification for 1xx codes) and waits for the final response. No errors are generated.
*   **Prioritization:** When the final response arrives, if it references resources that were already preloaded via 103, the browser uses its HTTP Cache rather than making a new network request.

**Caches (Intermediaries):**
*   **Non-storage:** 1xx informational responses are not cacheable. A cache will not store a 103 response.
*   **Forwarding:** Caches (like CDN edge nodes) are permitted to forward 1xx responses to the client if the connection allows it. 
*   **Buffering:** If a cache or proxy buffers the connection and does not support forwarding 1xx responses, the 103 is effectively dropped, and the client only receives the final 200 response. 
*   **HTTP/2 PUSH Alternative:** HTTP/2 Server Push was deprecated (e.g., removed in Chrome 106). Early Hints is the standardized, safer alternative because it leaves the initiation of the fetch to the browser (respecting browser cache state and network prioritization), rather than the server forcing resources down the wire.

### 4. Real-world deployment caveats

**HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2:**
*   **HTTP/1.1:** 103 Early Hints works over HTTP/1.1. However, because HTTP/1.1 does not support multiplexing, a browser can only use the hint to open a *new* parallel TCP connection for the preload. If the browser has already hit its maximum concurrent connection limit (usually 6 per origin) for the main document request, the preload is queued until a connection frees up, negating much of the latency benefit.
*   **HTTP/2 & HTTP/3:** Early Hints is significantly more effective over HTTP/2 and HTTP/3. Multiplexing allows the browser to initiate preloads on the *existing* connection using secondary streams without waiting for connection limits. (Note: The 103 status code is sent as a HEADERS frame with the `END_STREAM` flag set to false, followed by the final 200 response headers).

**Proxies and Intermediary Infrastructure:**
*   **Proxy Forwarding:** Many reverse proxies, load balancers, and CDNs historically buffer responses from the origin. If an origin sends a 103 and then waits to send a 200, a buffering proxy will hold the connection open to the origin but will not forward the 103 to the client. The client only receives the 200 at the end. Operators must configure proxies to stream or flush 1xx responses immediately.
*   **Connection Upgrades (CONNECT):** According to RFC 8297, a server MUST NOT send a 103 response if the client's request has a body and the server has not finished reading the body. Doing so could break HTTP semantics if the request is a `CONNECT` request or if the proxy expects the request body to be fully transmitted first.
*   **Duplicate Processing:** If a proxy attempts to inject its own `Link` headers into a 103 response, it must ensure it also injects them into the 200 response. If a browser preloads a resource via 103 but the 200 response lacks the corresponding `Link` header, the browser may throw a console warning or fail to apply the correct priority to the preloaded resource.
*   **CDN Support:** CDNs must explicitly support HTTP 103. If a CDN's edge logic processes origin responses sequentially, it may strip 1xx responses. Cloudflare, for instance, implemented specific "Early Hints" logic to cache the 103 `Link` headers at the edge and serve them to clients before the origin even responds, but this requires the origin to be properly configured to send them.

I'll structure the answer clearly with the function, an explanation of the regex (borrowing from Expert 1's pedagogical breakdown adapted to the lookahead), and the combined test cases.Here is the `parseDuration` function, followed by a detailed explanation and a comprehensive suite of test cases covering valid and invalid edge cases.

```typescript
/**
 * Parses a duration string (e.g., '1h30m15s') into total seconds.
 * Rules:
 * - Units: h (hours), m (minutes), s (seconds)
 * - Order: Must appear strictly in h -> m -> s order
 * - Values: Non-negative integers (plain '0' allowed; overflow like '60m' is valid)
 * - Returns null for any invalid format
 */
function parseDuration(s: string): number | null {
  // The regex uses a positive lookahead to ensure the string ends with a unit (h, m, or s)
  // and optional non-capturing groups to enforce strict h -> m -> s ordering.
  const match = s.match(/^(?=.*[hms]$)(?:(\d+)h)?(?:(\d+)m)?(?:(\d+)s)?$/);

  if (!match) {
    return null;
  }

  const hours   = match[1] ? parseInt(match[1], 10) : 0;
  const minutes = match[2] ? parseInt(match[2], 10) : 0;
  const seconds = match[3] ? parseInt(match[3], 10) : 0;

  return hours * 3600 + minutes * 60 + seconds;
}
```

### How the Regex Works
1. `^` and `$`: Anchors the match to the entire string, preventing partial matches (e.g., trailing/leading characters).
2. `(?=.*[hms]$)`: A positive lookahead that ensures the string ends with at least one valid unit letter (`h`, `m`, or `s`). This neatly rejects empty strings and bare numbers like `'10'` purely within the regex.
3. `(?:(\d+)h)?`: An optional non-capturing group for hours. `(\d+)` captures one or more digits, ensuring a value is present if the unit is used (rejecting bare `'h'`).
4. `(?:(\d+)m)?` and `(?:(\d+)s)?`: Follows the exact same logic for minutes and seconds, strictly in order. If a unit is omitted, the group is skipped. 

### Test Cases

```javascript
// --- Valid Cases ---
console.log(parseDuration('1h30m15s')); // 5415 (Standard full string)
console.log(parseDuration('45m'));      // 2700 (Single unit, middle)
console.log(parseDuration('90s'));      // 90   (Single unit, end)
console.log(parseDuration('2h'));       // 7200 (Single unit, start)
console.log(parseDuration('1h60m'));    // 7200 (Overflow minutes - explicitly valid per rules)
console.log(parseDuration('0h5s'));     // 5    (Zero value and skipped unit)
console.log(parseDuration('1h0s'));     // 3600 (Missing middle unit)
console.log(parseDuration('0h0m0s'));   // 0    (All zero values)
console.log(parseDuration('0h'));       // 0    (Standalone zero unit)
console.log(parseDuration('0m'));       // 0    (Standalone zero unit)
console.log(parseDuration('0s'));       // 0    (Standalone zero unit)
console.log(parseDuration('100h'));     // 360000 (Large value)

// --- Invalid Cases ---
console.log(parseDuration(''));         // null (Empty string)
console.log(parseDuration('1m1h'));     // null (Wrong order)
console.log(parseDuration('1.5h'));     // null (Decimal not allowed)
console.log(parseDuration('h'));        // null (Unit without value)
console.log(parseDuration('10'));       // null (Value without unit)
console.log(parseDuration('1h1h'));     // null (Duplicate unit)
```

This Python snippet contains two of the most famous Python "gotchas": mutable default arguments and late binding closures. 

Here is a precise explanation of both bugs and how to fix them.

### Bug 1: Mutable Default Arguments
```python
def add_item(item, bucket=[]):
```
**The Problem:** 
In Python, default argument values are evaluated **only once** when the function is defined, not each time the function is called. Because lists are mutable, the same list object is created and shared across all subsequent calls to `add_item` that omit the `bucket` argument. If you call `add_item('a')` and then `add_item('b')`, the second call will return `['a', 'b']` rather than `['b']`.

**The Fix:**
Use an immutable sentinel value (like `None`) as the default argument, and initialize the mutable object inside the function body.
```python
def add_item(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket
```

### Bug 2: Late Binding Closures
```python
for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda: print(i))
```
**The Problem:** 
Python closures (like `lambda` functions) capture variables *by reference* (the variable's name), not *by value*. The lambda looks up the value of `i` in the surrounding scope **at the time the lambda is called**, not when it is defined. By the time the second loop runs and calls the callbacks, the `for` loop has finished, and `i` is left with its final value: `2`. Therefore, the script will print `2` three times instead of `0`, `1`, and `2`.

**The Fix:**
You can force early binding by passing the current value of `i` as a default argument to the lambda. Default arguments are evaluated at definition time, so each lambda will capture its own snapshot of `i`.
```python
callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))
```

### The Fixed Code
Here is the completely corrected snippet:

```python
def add_item(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    # Bind the current value of 'i' as a default argument
    callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))

for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```
*(Output will now correctly be `0`, `1`, `2` on separate lines)*

1. **Mutable default argument**
2. **Late-binding closure in a loop**

---

## Bug 1: Mutable default argument

```python
def add_item(item, bucket=[]):
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket
```

### Problem
Default argument values are evaluated **once**, when the function is **defined**, not each time it is called.  
So `bucket=[]` creates a single list object and reuses it for every call that does not pass a `bucket`.

```python
add_item(1)   # returns [1]
add_item(2)   # returns [1, 2]   ← unexpected shared state

def add_item(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket
```

Now each call without an argument gets its own list.

---

## Bug 2: Late-binding closure

```python
callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda: print(i))
for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```

### Problem
The `lambda` does **not** capture the current value of `i`. It captures the **variable name `i`**, and looks up its value when the lambda is actually called. By that time the loop has finished and `i == 2`.

Output:

```text
2
2
2
```

Bind the current value of `i` as a default argument to the lambda. Default arguments are evaluated at definition time, so each lambda gets its own value:

callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))

for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```

Output:

```text
0
1
2
```

    return lambda: print(i)

callbacks = [make_callback(i) for i in range(3)]
```

or `functools.partial(print, i)`.

This code demonstrates two of the most common "gotchas" in Python: **Mutable Default Arguments** and **Late Binding Closures**.

### 1. Mutable Default Arguments
**The Bug:**
In the function `add_item(item, bucket=[])`, the default value for `bucket` is a list. In Python, default arguments are evaluated only **once** at the time the function is defined, not every time the function is called.

**The Consequence:**
If you call `add_item` multiple times without providing a `bucket`, every call will share the **same list object** in memory. Adding an item in one call will persist for all subsequent calls.

### 2. Late Binding Closures
**The Bug:**
Inside the loop `for i in range(3)`, the lambda function `lambda: print(i)` captures the variable `i`, not its value at that specific moment. This is known as **late binding**.

**The Consequence:**
By the time the `callbacks` are actually executed in the second loop, the first loop has finished and the variable `i` is left sitting at its final value (`2`). Therefore, every callback looks up `i`, sees it is `2`, and prints `2`. You get `2, 2, 2` instead of `0, 1, 2`.

---

### The Fixed Code

```python
# Fix 1: Use None as a default and initialize inside the function
def add_item(item, bucket=None):
    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

# Fix 2: Use a default argument to force early binding of the variable 'i'
callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    # By setting i=i, we create a local variable 'i' for the lambda 
    # that is assigned the value of the loop's 'i' at that instant.
    callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))

for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```

### Summary of Changes:
1.  **For `add_item`**: We changed the default to `None`. Inside the function, we check if `bucket is None` and create a new list there. This ensures a fresh list is created every time the function is called without an argument.
2.  **For `callbacks`**: We changed the lambda to `lambda i=i: print(i)`. This uses a default argument trick. Since default arguments are evaluated at definition time (as seen in Bug 1), it "saves" the current value of `i` into a local scope for each specific lambda.

There are **two distinct bugs** in this code, both classic Python pitfalls:

---

### Bug 1: **Mutable default argument** (in `add_item`)
```python

  ['apple']
  >>> add_item('banana')  # Same list reused!

      if bucket is None:
          bucket = []
      bucket.append(item)
      return bucket
  ```

---

### Bug 2: **Late binding in closures / variable capture** (in the lambda loop)
```python

for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda: print(i))  # ❌ BAD: late binding
for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```

  ```python
  >>> for cb in callbacks:
  ...     cb()
  2
  2
  2
  ```

- **Fix**: Capture the *current value* of `i` by using a default argument (evaluated at definition time):
  ```python
  callbacks = []
  for i in range(3):
      callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))  # ✅ Capture i by value
  for cb in callbacks:
      cb()
  # Output:
  # 0
  # 1
  # 2
  ```

---

### Why both are "classic":
1. **Mutable default arguments** are a gotcha because Python’s behavior is counterintuitive (most expect the default to be "reset" each call).
2. **Closure variable capture** is tricky because the lambda refers to the *name* `i`, not its value — a well-known Python gotcha (documented in the official FAQ).

```python

    if bucket is None:
        bucket = []
    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

callbacks = []
for i in range(3):

### Bug 1: Mutable default argument

```python
def add_item(item, bucket=[]):

    bucket.append(item)
    return bucket

# Corrected mutable-default behavior
print(add_item(1))        # [1]
print(add_item(2))        # [2]
print(add_item(3, [0]))   # [0, 3]

# Corrected closure behavior
callbacks = []
for i in range(3):
    callbacks.append(lambda i=i: print(i))

for cb in callbacks:
    cb()
```

Output:

```text
[1]
[2]
[0, 3]
0
1
2
```

---

### Summary

| Bug | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Mutable default argument | Default argument object is created once and shared | Use `None` default and create a new list inside |
| Late-binding closures | Lambda reads variable by name at call time | Bind value with `lambda i=i: ...`, or use a factory function / `functools.partial` |

Both bugs stem from Python evaluating names/defaults at unexpected times: mutable defaults are evaluated at function definition, and closures capture variables (not values), so loop variables must be explicitly bound if the closure will run after the loop has finished.

Here is a breakdown of PostgreSQL's `REPEATABLE READ` and `SERIALIZABLE` isolation levels, the anomalies they prevent, a concrete write-skew example, and the specific error handling required. ### 1. Anomalies Prevented In PostgreSQL, `REPEATABLE READ` is implemented using Snapshot Isolation, and `SERIALIZABLE` is implemented using Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI). Because of this, PostgreSQL's `REPEATABLE READ` is actually stricter than the SQL standard requires (it prevents phantom reads). However, it still allows serialization anomalies like write skew. | Anomaly | `REPEATABLE READ` | `SERIALIZABLE` | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **Dirty Read** (Reading uncommitted data) | Prevented | Prevented | | **Non-repeatable Read** (Row changes between reads) | Prevented | Prevented | | **Phantom Read** (New rows appear between reads) | Prevented | Prevented | | **Serialization Anomaly / Write Skew** | **Allowed** | **Prevented** | ### 2. Concrete Write-Skew Example Write skew occurs when two transactions read overlapping data, and based on what they read, they modify *disjoint* data. Because they don't write to the same rows, they don't block each other, but the resulting state violates a business rule. **Scenario:** A hospital has a rule that at least one doctor must be on call at all times. Currently, Alice and Bob are both on call. ```sql CREATE TABLE doctors (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, on_call BOOLEAN); INSERT INTO doctors VALUES (1, 'Alice', true), (2, 'Bob', true); ``` **Transaction Timeline (Both transactions run concurrently):** | Time | Transaction 1 (Alice goes home) | Transaction 2 (Bob goes home) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | | 2 | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;`
*(Returns: 2)* | | | 3 | | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;`
*(Returns: 2)* | | 4 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | | | 5 | | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` | | 6 | `COMMIT;` | | | 7 | | `COMMIT;` | **Result under `REPEATABLE READ`:** Both transactions commit successfully. The table now has zero doctors on call. The business rule is violated. **Result under `SERIALIZABLE`:** If both transactions are run with `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;`, PostgreSQL tracks the read/write dependencies (SSI). Because both transactions read the count and both mutate the set of "on call" doctors, PostgreSQL detects a dangerous structure. Transaction 1 commits successfully. When Transaction 2 attempts to `COMMIT;`, PostgreSQL aborts it and throws an error: `ERROR: could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions` ### 3. The Exact SQLSTATE Code for Retries Because `SERIALIZABLE` actively detects anomalies and aborts transactions, applications using this isolation level must be designed to handle failures and retry the transaction from the beginning. The exact `SQLSTATE` code you must catch and retry on is: **`40001`** (`serialization_failure`) *(Note: You should also typically catch `40P01` (`deadlock_detected`) at the same time, as both require the same application-level retry logic).*

### Anomalies prevented

| Anomaly | PostgreSQL `REPEATABLE READ` | PostgreSQL `SERIALIZABLE` |
|---|---|---|
| Dirty read | prevented | prevented |
| Non-repeatable read | prevented (transaction snapshot) | prevented |
| Phantom read | prevented (transaction snapshot) | prevented |
| Lost update on the **same row** | prevented (row conflict → `40001`) | prevented |
| Write skew / serialization anomalies | **NOT prevented** | **prevented** (by aborting one txn) |

So `REPEATABLE READ` is effectively *Snapshot Isolation*: each transaction sees a stable snapshot, but it can still commit changes that are mutually inconsistent with another concurrent transaction. `SERIALIZABLE` uses Serializable Snapshot Isiliation (SSI) to ensure the committed result is equivalent to some serial order.

---

### Concrete write-skew example

Schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE doctors (
    id int PRIMARY KEY,
    name text,
    on_call boolean NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO doctors VALUES
    (1, 'Alice', true),
    (2, 'Bob',   true);
```

Business rule: at least one doctor must be on call.

**Session A** wants to take Alice off call only if someone else is on call:

```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT count(*) FROM doctors
 WHERE on_call = true AND id <> 1;   -- sees Bob: count = 1
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 1;
COMMIT;
```

**Session B** wants to take Bob off call only if someone else is on call:

```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT count(*) FROM doctors
 WHERE on_call = true AND id <> 2;   -- sees Alice: count = 1
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 2;
COMMIT;
```

Both snapshots showed the other doctor on call, so both transactions commit. Final state:

```sql
SELECT * FROM doctors;
-- id | name  | on_call
-- ----+-------+---------
--   1 | Alice | f
--   2 | Bob   | f
```

Both doctors are now off call — a **write skew** anomaly that violates the business rule.

If you run the same two transactions with:

```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
```

answers.solo-deepseek-flash:
## PostgreSQL `SERIALIZABLE` vs `REPEATABLE READ`

### Anomalies prevented by each level

| Isolation Level | Dirty Read | Non‑repeatable Read | Phantom Read | Write Skew | Other Serialization Anomalies |
|----------------|------------|----------------------|--------------|------------|-----------------------------|
| `REPEATABLE READ` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `SERIALIZABLE`    | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

- **`REPEATABLE READ`** in PostgreSQL uses **Snapshot Isolation (SI)**. It prevents dirty reads, non‑repeatable reads, and phantom reads because each transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the data at the start. However, SI does **not** prevent **write skew** or other serialization anomalies (e.g., read‑only anomalies, SI‑lost updates).
- **`SERIALIZABLE`** uses **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)**, which detects conflicts between concurrent transactions and aborts one of them to guarantee true serializability. It prevents all anomalies, including write skew.

---

### Concrete Write‑Skew Example

**Schema**  
A table `doctors` with a constraint that at least one doctor must be on call at any time.

```sql
CREATE TABLE doctors (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    on_call BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false
);

-- Initial state: two doctors on call
INSERT INTO doctors VALUES (1, 'Alice', true);
INSERT INTO doctors VALUES (2, 'Bob', true);
```

**Two concurrent transactions (both at `REPEATABLE READ`):**

| Time | Transaction T1 | Transaction T2 |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` |
| 2 | `SELECT * FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` → sees Alice, Bob | `SELECT * FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` → sees Alice, Bob |
| 3 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` |
| 4 | `COMMIT;` | `COMMIT;` |

**Result:** Both transactions commit. The final state is `on_call = false` for both doctors – **no doctor on call**, violating the business rule. This is a **write skew** anomaly.

**Why `REPEATABLE READ` allows it:**  
Each transaction reads the same snapshot (both doctors on call). They update **different rows** (different primary keys), so no row‑level lock conflict occurs. Snapshot isolation does not detect that the *set* of rows read by one transaction overlaps with the *set* of rows written by the other.

**Why `SERIALIZABLE` aborts it:**  
Under SSI, the system tracks read‑write conflicts between transactions. T1’s read of the `on_call` rows creates a **predicate‑based dependency** with T2’s write to one of those rows (and vice versa). When both try to commit, SSI detects a **dangerous structure** and aborts one of them (e.g., T2) with a serialization failure.

---

### SQLSTATE Code for Retry

When a `SERIALIZABLE` transaction is aborted due to a serialization anomaly, PostgreSQL returns:

```
ERROR:  could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions
DETAIL:  Reason code: Canceled on identification as a pivot, during commit attempt.
HINT:  The transaction might succeed if you retry.
```

The corresponding **SQLSTATE** is **`40001`** (`serialization_failure`).

**Application retry logic** should catch this error code and re‑run the entire transaction from the beginning. Example in Python (using `psycopg2`):

```python
from psycopg2 import errors

while True:
    try:
        # execute transaction
        conn.commit()
        break
    except errors.SerializationFailure:
        conn.rollback()
        # wait a short random time before retrying
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.5))
```

**Important:** Only `SERIALIZABLE` transactions can receive `40001` for write‑skew scenarios. `REPEATABLE READ` will never abort for this anomaly – it will silently allow the violation.

While PostgreSQL's `REPEATABLE READ` is stronger than the SQL standard (it prevents Phantoms), it still allows **Write Skew**. ### 1. Anomalies Prevented | Anomaly | REPEATABLE READ | SERIALIZABLE | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Dirty Read** | Prevented | Prevented | | **Non-repeatable Read** | Prevented | Prevented | | **Phantom Read** | **Prevented** (unique to PG) | Prevented | | **Serialization Anomaly (Write Skew)** | **Allowed** | **Prevented** | --- ### 2. Concrete Example: Write Skew Write skew occurs when two transactions read the same data, but modify **different** sets of rows in a way that violates a business constraint that would have been maintained if the transactions ran one after the other. #### The Scenario: The Doctor On-Call Rule A hospital requires at least one doctor to be on call at all times. Currently, two doctors (Alice and Bob) are on call. **Setup:** ```sql CREATE TABLE doctors ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, on_call BOOLEAN ); INSERT INTO doctors (name, on_call) VALUES ('Alice', true), ('Bob', true); ``` #### Under REPEATABLE READ (Fails Integrity) Both doctors try to go off duty at the exact same time. | Time | Transaction A (Alice) | Transaction B (Bob) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | T1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | | T2 | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` (Result: 2) | | | T3 | | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` (Result: 2) | | T4 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | | | T5 | | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` | | T6 | `COMMIT;` (Success) | | | T7 | | `COMMIT;` (Success) | **Result:** Both transactions commit. Zero doctors are now on call. The business rule is broken. This happens because the updates targeted different rows, so no row-level lock conflict occurred. #### Under SERIALIZABLE (Maintains Integrity) PostgreSQL uses **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)** to track dependencies. | Time | Transaction A (Alice) | Transaction B (Bob) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | T1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;` | | T2 | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` | | | T3 | | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` | | T4 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | | | T5 | | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` | | T6 | `COMMIT;` (Success) | | | T7 | | **`COMMIT;` -> ERROR: could not serialize access** | **Result:** PostgreSQL detects that Transaction B relied on a "snapshot" of data that was changed by Transaction A (a read/write dependency circle). To ensure the result is the same as if they ran serially, it forces one to fail. --- ### 3. The SQLSTATE Code When using `SERIALIZABLE` (or occasionally `REPEATABLE READ` during concurrent updates to the same row), the application must be prepared to catch a specific error and retry the transaction from the beginning. The exact SQLSTATE code is: **`40001`** (`serialization_failure`) **Application logic pseudocode:** ```python while True: try: execute_transaction() break # Success! except DatabaseError as e: if e.sqlstate == '40001': continue # Retry the transaction else: raise # Different error, handle normally ```
answers.solo-qwen-coder:
### Comparison of PostgreSQL Isolation Levels: SERIALIZABLE vs REPEATABLE READ

#### 1. **Anomaly Prevention**

| Anomaly                     | REPEATABLE READ | SERIALIZABLE |
|-----------------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Dirty Read**              | ❌ Prevented    | ❌ Prevented |
| **Non-Repeatable Read**     | ✅ Prevented    | ✅ Prevented |
| **Phantom Read**            | ⚠️ *Not fully prevented* (see note below) | ✅ Prevented |
| **Write Skew (a subset of Anomalous Interactions / Serialization Anomalies)** | ❌ **Allowed** | ✅ Prevented |

> **Note on Phantom Reads in REPEATABLE READ**:  
> In PostgreSQL, **phantom reads are not prevented** under REPEATABLE READ — it only guarantees that rows *already visible* in a query’s result set remain consistent across queries in the same transaction. New rows satisfying a `WHERE` clause can appear on subsequent `SELECT`s (e.g., if another transaction inserts them and commits before your transaction sees them).  
> SERIALIZABLE prevents *all* serialization anomalies, including phantom-like inconsistencies caused by concurrent writes affecting logical consistency (e.g., write skew).

---

#### 2. **Concrete Write-Skew Example**

**Scenario**: Two bank branches (A and B) must ensure **at least one branch always has ≥ $100**.  
- Initial state: Branch A = $150, Branch B = $150  
- Constraint: `A ≥ 100 OR B ≥ 100` must *always* hold.

**Transaction 1 (T1)**:
```sql
-- Start REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;  -- or SERIALIZABLE

-- Read both balances
SELECT amount FROM accounts WHERE branch = 'A';  -- sees $150
SELECT amount FROM accounts WHERE branch = 'B';  -- sees $150

-- Decide: transfer $60 from A to B (A→90, B→210), satisfying constraint (B still ≥100)
UPDATE accounts SET amount = amount - 60 WHERE branch = 'A';  -- tentative write
-- (no actual write to disk until commit — writes are in write-set)
```

**Transaction 2 (T2)**:
```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;  -- or SERIALIZABLE

-- Simultaneously, T2 does the reverse: transfer $60 from B to A
SELECT amount FROM accounts WHERE branch = 'A';  -- sees $150 (consistent snapshot)
SELECT amount FROM accounts WHERE branch = 'B';  -- sees $150

UPDATE accounts SET amount = amount - 60 WHERE branch = 'B';  -- tentative write
```

**Execution Timeline** (both run concurrently, T1 and T2 both commit):
- T1: reads A=150, B=150 → plans A−60, B+0  
- T2: reads A=150, B=150 → plans B−60, A+0  
- T1 commits first (e.g., sets A=90, B=150)  
- T2 commits second (sets B=90, A=150) — *or* vice versa.

**Final State**:  
- If T1 first: A=90, B=210 → constraint still OK  
- If T2 first: A=210, B=90 → constraint still OK  
- BUT if **both apply their *read* values and write unilaterally**, and due to timing, final state becomes:
  - **A = 90, B = 90** → violates constraint! (neither ≥ 100)

> How? PostgreSQL’s `UPDATE` uses **MVCC + snapshot read**, but in **REPEATABLE READ**, the write in each transaction is based on *stale* values (both read A=150, B=150), and both commit *without seeing each other's writes*, leading to a lost update + violation of logical constraint.

**Why REPEATABLE READ allows it**:  
- REPEATABLE READ only prevents inconsistent *read* anomalies (dirty/non-repeatable reads), but does **not enforce global serialization ordering** of transactions whose writes *interact* based on overlapping reads.

**Why SERIALIZABLE prevents it**:  
- PostgreSQL’s SERIALIZABLE detects this *conflict graph cycle* and aborts **one** of the transactions (T1 or T2) with an error during commit, ensuring the final state is consistent (i.e., one transaction succeeds, the other fails).

---

#### 3. **Exact SQLSTATE Code for Retry**

The application **must retry** when the SERIALIZABLE transaction fails with:

```
SQLSTATE: 40001
Error message: `ERROR:  could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions`
```

> ✅ **Retriable error code**: `'40001'` (standard SQLSTATE for `serialization_failure`)

**Best practice for client code**:
```python
# Pseudocode (Python psycopg2 example)
try:
    with conn:
        cur.execute("BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;")
        # ... your logic ...
        # If commit fails with 40001, retry
except psycopg2.errors.SerializationFailure:
    # retry (ideally with exponential backoff & limit)
    # ... retry logic ...
```

---

### Summary Table

| Feature                     | REPEATABLE READ | SERIALIZABLE |
|----------------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Prevents dirty reads       | ✅              | ✅           |
| Prevents non-repeatable reads | ✅           | ✅           |
| Prevents phantom reads*    | ❌ (partial)    | ✅           |
| Prevents write skew        | ❌              | ✅           |
| Serialization anomaly safety | No (weak isolation) | Yes (strong) |
| Retry on SQLSTATE          | —               | **40001**    |

> *PostgreSQL implements REPEATABLE READ using a single snapshot for the transaction — phantoms due to concurrent `INSERT`s *can* appear.*

Drafting response:### 1. Anomalies Prevented

PostgreSQL’s implementation of `REPEATABLE READ` uses **Snapshot Isolation**, which is stricter than the SQL standard requires. It prevents not only non-repeatable reads but also phantom reads and read skew. 

The primary differentiator between the two levels is the handling of **serialization anomalies (write skew)**, which `SERIALIZABLE` prevents using **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)**.

| Anomaly | READ COMMITTED | REPEATABLE READ | SERIALIZABLE |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Dirty Read** | Prevented | Prevented | Prevented |
| **Non-repeatable Read** | Allowed | Prevented | Prevented |
| **Phantom Read** | Allowed | **Prevented** | Prevented |
| **Read Skew** | Allowed | Prevented | Prevented |
| **Serialization Anomaly (Write Skew)** | Allowed | **Allowed** | **Prevented** |

*(Note: In many other databases like MySQL/InnoDB, `REPEATABLE READ` does not prevent Phantom Reads. In PostgreSQL, it does.)*

---

### 2. Concrete Write-Skew Example

Write skew occurs when two concurrent transactions read the same data, but modify different sets of rows in a way that violates a business logic constraint that would have been maintained if the transactions ran one after the other. 

Because the transactions modify **different rows**, no row-level lock conflict occurs under `REPEATABLE READ`, allowing both to commit. Under `SERIALIZABLE`, PostgreSQL tracks these read/write dependencies using "predicate locks" (SIREAD locks) to detect the conflict.

#### The Scenario: "At least one doctor on call"
A hospital requires that **at least one** doctor must be "on call" at all times. Currently, two doctors (Alice and Bob) are on call, and both want to take a break.

**Setup:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE doctors (
    name TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    on_call BOOLEAN NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO doctors VALUES ('Alice', true), ('Bob', true);
```

#### Execution under REPEATABLE READ (Fails to protect logic)
| Time | Transaction A (Alice) | Transaction B (Bob) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| T1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` |
| T2 | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` (Result: 2) | |
| T3 | | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` (Result: 2) |
| T4 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | |
| T5 | | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` |
| T6 | `COMMIT;` (Success) | |
| T7 | | `COMMIT;` (Success) |

**Result:** Both transactions commit. No rows are currently "on call." The business rule is violated. 

#### Execution under SERIALIZABLE (Prevents the anomaly)
| Time | Transaction A (Alice) | Transaction B (Bob) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| T1 | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;` | `BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;` |
| T2 | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` | |
| T3 | | `SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true;` |
| T4 | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';` | |
| T5 | | `UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';` |
| T6 | `COMMIT;` (Success) | |
| T7 | | **`ERROR: could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions`** |

**Result:** PostgreSQL detects that Transaction B read data that was subsequently modified by Transaction A in a way that would have changed the result of B's initial query. It forces Transaction B to abort at `COMMIT`. (Note: While SSI typically detects the conflict at COMMIT as shown above, the abort can also occur on a subsequent read or write statement within the transaction).

*(Another valid variant of this write-skew example is an "at most one doctor on call" rule, where two off-duty doctors concurrently check that count=0 and both set their own status to `true`.)*

---

### 3. SQLSTATE Code for Retries

When using `SERIALIZABLE`, the application must be prepared to catch a specific error and retry. 

The exact **SQLSTATE** code for a serialization failure is:
### **`40001`** 

When this error occurs, the application must retry the **entire transaction from `BEGIN`**, not just the failed statement. 

```python
while True:
    try:
        # 1. Start Transaction (ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE)
        # 2. Perform Reads and Writes
        # 3. Commit
        break # Success!
    except DatabaseError as e:
        if e.sqlstate == '40001':
            continue # Retry the entire transaction
        else:
            raise # Other error
```

**Note:** You might also occasionally encounter **`40P01`** (`deadlock_detected`). While distinct from `40001`, robust applications typically treat both `40001` and `40P01` as retryable events.
answers.fusion-agents:
### 1. Anomalies Prevented by Each Isolation Level

In PostgreSQL, the implementation of concurrency control (MVCC + Snapshot Isolation) makes its isolation levels stricter than the SQL standard requires. 

**PostgreSQL REPEATABLE READ (Snapshot Isolation) prevents:**
*   **Dirty Read:** Reading uncommitted data from another transaction.
*   **Non-repeatable Read:** Getting different results for the same query within the same transaction because another transaction committed an update.
*   **Phantom Read:** Getting different numbers of rows for the same predicate query because another transaction inserted or deleted matching rows.

**PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE (Serializable Snapshot Isolation - SSI) prevents:**
*   *All of the above, plus...*
*   **Write Skew:** Two concurrent transactions read overlapping data, make decisions based on what they read, and write to disjoint sets of data. Because they write to different rows, they don't conflict at the row level (no deadlock), but the combined result violates the business logic of the database.
*   **Serialization Anomalies:** Any anomaly where the result of executing concurrent transactions is impossible to achieve if they were run one at a time in some sequential order.

---

### 2. Concrete Write-Skew Example

**The Business Rule:** 
A hospital has an on-call system. The business rule is that **at least one doctor must be on call** at all times. 

**The Setup:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE doctors (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    on_call BOOLEAN
);

INSERT INTO doctors (id, name, on_call) VALUES 
(1, 'Alice', true),
(2, 'Bob', true);
```

**The Execution (Under REPEATABLE READ):**

Suppose Alice and Bob are the only two doctors on call, and they are both exhausted. They concurrently try to take themselves off call.

*Transaction 1 (Alice)*
```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
-- T1 reads: There are 2 doctors on call. 
-- Decision: 2 - 1 = 1. It is safe for me to go off call.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true; -- Returns 2

-- Meanwhile, Transaction 2 runs...

UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';
COMMIT;
```

*Transaction 2 (Bob)*
```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
-- T2 reads: There are 2 doctors on call (snapshot taken before T1 committed).
-- Decision: 2 - 1 = 1. It is safe for me to go off call.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true; -- Returns 2

UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';
COMMIT;
```

**The Result under REPEATABLE READ:**
Both transactions successfully commit. The database now has `0` doctors on call. The business rule is broken. Because Alice and Bob updated *different* rows, PostgreSQL's snapshot isolation did not detect a conflict.

**The Execution (Under SERIALIZABLE):**

If we repeat the exact same sequence under `SERIALIZABLE`, PostgreSQL tracks the read/write dependencies using Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI). 

1. T1 reads the count of on-call doctors (reads Alice and Bob's rows).
2. T2 reads the count of on-call doctors (reads Alice and Bob's rows).
3. T1 updates Alice's row.
4. T2 updates Bob's row.
5. When T2 attempts to `COMMIT`, PostgreSQL detects a dangerous structure: T1 read something T2 is writing, T2 read something T1 is writing, and both are writing. This is a "dangerous structure" indicating a write skew.

PostgreSQL will **abort** Transaction 2 and roll it back, outputting:
`ERROR: could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions`

---

### 3. The Exact SQLSTATE Code for Retries

When a transaction fails under `SERIALIZABLE` due to a detected anomaly (like the write skew above), the application is expected to handle the failure by retrying the transaction from the beginning.

The application must look for the exact SQLSTATE code:
**`40001`**

*(Note: Sometimes, concurrent serializable transactions can fail even earlier—during the execution of a statement rather than at commit—due to a slightly different low-level SSI conflict. In those rare cases, PostgreSQL might return SQLSTATE `40P01` (deadlock detected). A robust retry loop for PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE should handle both `40001` and `40P01`.)*
answers.self-kimi:
## Anomalies prevented in PostgreSQL

| Anomaly | `REPEATABLE READ` | `SERIALIZABLE` |
|---|---|---|
| Dirty read | Prevented | Prevented |
| Non‑repeatable read | Prevented | Prevented |
| Phantom read | Prevented in PostgreSQL (each transaction uses a single snapshot) | Prevented |
| Lost update on the **same row** | Prevented (aborts with `40001`) | Prevented |
| **Write skew / serialization anomalies across different rows** | **Allowed** | **Prevented** |

In short:

- `REPEATABLE READ` in PostgreSQL is **Snapshot Isolation**: your transaction sees a stable, consistent snapshot from the moment it begins, so read anomalies disappear. But two concurrent transactions can each read a shared condition and then write **different rows**, together violating an invariant.
- `SERIALIZABLE` uses **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)**: PostgreSQL tracks read/write dependencies and aborts a transaction if it could produce a non‑serializable result, so write skew is blocked.

---

## Concrete write‑skew example: “At least one doctor on call”

### Setup

```sql
CREATE TABLE doctors (
    name text PRIMARY KEY,
    on_call boolean NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO doctors VALUES
    ('Alice', true),
    ('Bob',   true);

-- Business rule: at least one doctor must remain on call.
```

### Under `REPEATABLE READ`

Run these two transactions concurrently:

```sql
-- Session 1
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call;   -- sees 2
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Alice';
COMMIT;                                        -- succeeds
```

```sql
-- Session 2
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT count(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call;   -- also sees 2
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE name = 'Bob';
COMMIT;                                        -- succeeds
```

Both sessions read that two doctors are on call, so each thinks it is safe to go off call. Because they update **different rows**, PostgreSQL detects no row‑level conflict, and **both commit**.

Final state:

```sql
SELECT * FROM doctors;
-- Alice | false
-- Bob   | false
```

No doctor is on call — the invariant is violated. This is **write skew**, which `REPEATABLE READ` allows.

### Under `SERIALIZABLE`

Use the same two sessions, but with:

```sql
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
```

The first transaction to commit succeeds. The second is aborted by SSI because the two transactions formed a read/write dependency cycle:

```text
ERROR:  could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions
DETAIL:  Reason code: Canceled on identification as a pivot, during commit.
HINT:  The transaction might succeed if retried.
SQLSTATE: 40001
```

So `SERIALIZABLE` prevents the write skew by aborting one transaction and forcing the application to retry.

---

## Exact SQLSTATE to retry on

For serialization failures, including write‑skew aborts under `SERIALIZABLE`, the SQLSTATE is:

```text
40001
```

Error name: `serialization_failure`

Application code should catch `SQLSTATE '40001'` and retry the **entire transaction** from the beginning.

> Note: deadlocks are a different error and use SQLSTATE `40P01` (`deadlock_detected`). The specific code for serializable‑isolation conflicts is **40001**.

answers.solo-glm:
A cache stampede on a key handling 10,000 RPS with a 200ms recompute time is a severe scenario. If the cache expires and all requests simultaneously miss, you will generate roughly **2,000 concurrent backend requests** (10,000 RPS × 0.2s). This will almost certainly overload the database or downstream API.

Here is a comparison of the three primary mitigations for this scenario, focusing on tail latency, complexity, and ideal use cases.

---

### 1. Per-Key Mutex / Single-Flight
**Mechanism:** When a cache miss occurs, the first request acquires a distributed lock (e.g., Redis `SETNX`). It computes the value (200ms), writes it to the cache, and releases the lock. All other concurrent requests wait (polling or subscribing) for the lock to be released and then read the newly cached value.

*   **Tail-latency behavior: Poor to Moderate.**
    *   The first request experiences the full 200ms latency.
    *   All concurrent requests waiting on the lock also experience ~200ms latency (plus lock overhead).
    *   **Danger:** At 10k RPS, thousands of requests will block waiting for 200ms. In synchronous architectures (like Tomcat/Java or PHP-FPM), this can exhaust thread pools and cause cascading timeouts. Your p99 and p999 latency will spike to 200ms+.
*   **Complexity: Medium to High.**
    *   Implementing distributed locking is notoriously tricky. You must handle lock timeouts (what if the node holding the lock crashes?), retries, and thundering herd on lock release.
    *   Requires a highly available lock provider (e.g., Redis, etcd).
*   **When to choose:**
    *   When serving **stale data is absolutely unacceptable** (e.g., financial balances, security tokens), and your architecture is asynchronous (non-blocking) so it can tolerate thousands of requests waiting on a lock without exhausting threads.

---

### 2. Probabilistic Early Refresh (XFetch)
**Mechanism:** Instead of waiting for the TTL to expire, clients probabilistically decide to recompute the value *before* it expires. The probability increases exponentially as the TTL approaches zero. Formula: $P = 1 - \exp(-\frac{\Delta}{\beta})$, where $\Delta$ is the time since the cache was supposed to expire and $\beta$ is a scaling factor.

*   **Tail-latency behavior: Good, but uneven.**
    *   Because recomputes happen in the background or before expiry, most users still get cache hits (< 1ms latency).
    *   The unlucky user who wins the probability lottery triggers the recompute and experiences the 200ms latency.
    *   **Danger:** Because it is probabilistic, at the exact moment of expiry, 2-3 nodes might win the lottery simultaneously, causing a tiny stampede. Furthermore, if no one wins the lottery before expiry, a standard stampede still occurs.
*   **Complexity: Low to Medium.**
    *   The logic is just a few lines of math in the application code.
    *   However, to completely eliminate the mini-stampede, XFetch is almost always paired with a Per-Key Mutex (so only the node that wins the lottery actually does the 200ms recompute).
*   **When to choose:**
    *   When you have a strict TTL but want to avoid the strict synchronous waiting of a pure mutex. It is excellent for **distributing the recompute load over time**, smoothing out the backend workload, and it requires no stale data logic.

---

### 3. Stale-While-Revalidate (SWR)
**Mechanism:** The cache stores two TTLs: a "fresh" TTL and a "stale" TTL. If a request hits during the fresh TTL, it serves the data normally. Once the fresh TTL expires but the stale TTL hasn't, the system serves the slightly stale data to the user instantly (< 1ms) and asynchronously triggers a background job to recompute the 200ms value.

*   **Tail-latency behavior: Excellent (Flat).**
    *   **No user ever waits for the 200ms recompute.** Every request (except the very first cold start) gets a sub-millisecond cache hit.
    *   p99 and p999 latencies remain completely flat, protecting the system from timeout cascades.
*   **Complexity: Low (Cache side) / Medium (App side).**
    *   Many modern caches (CDNs, Varnish, Redis with modules) support SWR natively.
    *   In custom implementations, you need a mechanism to trigger an asynchronous background job (e.g., putting a message on a queue, or using a non-blocking async thread in your app) and a way to store the stale data alongside the fresh data.
*   **When to choose:**
    *   When serving slightly stale data is acceptable (e.g., user profiles, product catalogs, aggregate metrics, recommendation feeds).
    *   This is generally the **industry-standard best practice** for high-traffic hot keys because it perfectly isolates the user experience from backend recompute latency.

---

### Summary Matrix

| Mitigation | Backend Load Spikes? | User Tail Latency (p99) | Can Serve Stale Data? | Implementation Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Per-Key Mutex** | No (1 recompute) | **200ms+** (Thread blocking) | No | High (Distributed locks) |
| **XFetch** | Low (Smoothed out) | **200ms** (For 1 unlucky user) | No | Medium (Math + optional lock) |
| **SWR** | No (Async background) | **< 1ms** (Flat) | Yes | Medium (Async job infra) |

### Final Recommendation
For a 10k RPS key with a heavy 200ms recompute:
1.  **Default to Stale-While-Revalidate (SWR)** if the business logic tolerates 200ms–1s of stale data. It is the only method that guarantees your users won't feel the 200ms latency spike, and it prevents thread exhaustion on your web servers.
2.  Use **XFetch** if you cannot serve stale data but want to avoid the massive thread-blocking of a pure mutex. It shifts the recompute to slightly *before* expiration, though one user will still pay the 200ms latency penalty.
3.  Avoid pure **Per-Key Mutex / Single-Flight** at this scale unless you are using a fully asynchronous, non-blocking runtime (like Go, Node.js, or Vert.x). In a blocking architecture, 10,000 RPS waiting on a lock for 200ms will take down your application servers via thread-pool starvation.
